source_title: Q&A – Expressing 'Anger At', Playing with Depression, Alone But Not Lonely, Following Connection, Desire and Patience Through Deep Work

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: We just got our Spotify statistics back for the year, and we are in the top 1% most shared podcasts for 2022 globally. We're in the top 5% most followed globally already. We've had over 500% growth of listeners over the past year. Another stat we got from Spotify is that the listener type of our listeners is the devotee, which means our listeners tend to binge the podcast and listen to the same episodes over and over because they really love them. I'm so grateful that people really like what we're doing and that people share it so much.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: I want to just thank everybody for sharing the podcast. It's almost ridiculous—I'm just dumbfounded that people are into it like this. We listen to every podcast before it gets published and I'm like, oh, that's good, I would enjoy listening to that. But still, somehow or another in my brain I'm just like, wow, this is happening. People dig it, which is really cool. On that note of gratitude and thank you, we're going to take this episode as an opportunity to answer some questions that people have asked.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: The first question is from Laura. She says she's noticed herself changing a lot through the view practice as well as other growth practices. She still struggles with the concept of not having anger at. In theory it seems very correct and healthy, but she's not sure what to do when the feeling of anger at does arise. She's curious if you can speak to the difference between feeling anger at and expressing it. How can I be both welcoming to my feelings but also know that's not an okay feeling?

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: I don't know if it's possible to never feel angry at, but I don't know anybody who doesn't have that experience. There's no way I would want you to repress that internal emotional state. I apologize in whatever way I've miscommunicated so that you feel like there's some part of your emotional state that you aren't allowed to love or accept. What I'm saying is the action of getting angry at somebody without permission. Sometimes Tara and I will give each other permission—we'll see the anger and be like, yeah, it's okay. When we're coaching people or in retreats, we'll say, yeah, get angry at us, it's fine. However, without permission, then it's just manipulation. Basically what's happening is somebody feels out of control. If they get angry at somebody else, it's their bid for control. That usually means controlling the other person.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: One way to tell is if my anger is moving through me, and as a result or as an action from that anger I'm trying to change the other person—change their beliefs or their story, be above them, dominate them, make them take an action, make them feel scared, make them back away, make them be anything—then that's an example of manipulating. What I've been finding through this work is that if I'm allowing that anger to move in me and I'm just feeling it, it's like it's my anger, it's not their anger, it's not for them, it's for me. The anger is for myself to feel that feeling and let it burn through whatever story I have so that I can find my clarity. And then from there I can go to the person.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: The weird thing is that though most of the time when we are angry we are angry at something, the anger is all about us. The anger is us feeling overwhelmed, or the anger is us needing to draw a boundary, or the anger is us learning how to not be passive aggressive. That doesn't mean you're responsible for getting angry—if you're angry you can beat yourself up, that's not what I'm saying. The anger is for us to learn. It's not about the other person. I highly recommend for people who are self-abusive to move the anger out. If that's at somebody or at God or at me or at you, do it. Just do it either with permission or do it somewhere where that person can't hear you or know that it's happening because it's not their job to hold your anger. They can do it, but it's not their job.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: Next question is from Eric. He says he finds that he struggles way more when trying to connect in group situations. He knows that staying in view is important, though for him it's harder to be both attentive in the conversation and present in his body without ignoring one or the other.
